title: How to Stop Shrinking Yourself to Make Others Comfortable
meta_description: Discover why you shrink yourself to make others comfortable and how to stop. A direct guide to reclaiming your power without apology.
og_title: How to Stop Shrinking Yourself to Make Others Comfortable
og_description: Discover why you shrink yourself to make others comfortable and how to stop. A direct guide to reclaiming your power without apology.
body:
You Know That Feeling, Don't You?

You're in a conversation, and something shifts. You feel the other person's energy tighten, their mood cloud over, or their attention drift. And before you even think about it, you start to shrink. You make yourself smaller. You soften your voice. You hold back what you really wanted to say. You laugh at a joke that isn't funny. You agree when you don't agree. You apologize for taking up space.

It happens so fast, so automatically, that you barely notice it's happening. But afterward, you feel it. That hollow ache in your chest. That quiet, gnawing resentment. That voice that whispers, Why did I do that again?

You're not alone. This is one of the most common struggles for people who feel things deeply. You've been told your whole life that you're "too much" or "too sensitive" or "too intense." So you learned to dial it down. To hide your brightness. To make yourself palatable. To be the version of you that doesn't ruffle feathers.

But here's the thing no one tells you: shrinking yourself doesn't actually make anyone comfortable. It just makes you invisible. And it drains the life out of you in the process.

I'm Kalesh. I'm a teacher of psychic development and intuitive sensitivity, and I've worked with hundreds of people who have this exact struggle. They're empaths, intuitives, deep feelers. They're the ones who walk into a room and instantly know how everyone is feeling. They're the ones who absorb the mood like a sponge. And they're the ones who have been conditioned to believe that their sensitivity is a problem to be managed, not a gift to be claimed.

Let me be clear: nothing is wrong with you. Your nervous system is calibrated for a wider range than most. You pick up on things other people miss. You feel things other people numb out from. That's not a flaw. That's a feature. But if you don't learn how to work with it, it will run your life.

So let's talk about how to stop shrinking. How to stop making yourself small so other people can feel big. How to stop apologizing for your presence. And how to start taking up the space you were born to fill.

"You weren't born small. You were taught to be small. And what was taught can be unlearned."

Why You Started Shrinking in the First Place

Let's get real about this. You didn't just wake up one day and decide to be a people-pleaser. You learned to shrink because it kept you safe. Maybe you grew up in a house where your feelings were dismissed, where you were told you were "too dramatic" or "too emotional." Maybe you had a parent who couldn't handle your intensity, so you learned to hide it. Maybe you were bullied or rejected for being different, and you figured out that blending in was the safest option.

It's a survival strategy. And it worked. It got you through. But survival strategies have a shelf life. What kept you safe as a child will suffocate you as an adult. The problem is, your nervous system doesn't know the difference between a real threat and a perceived one. So when you feel someone's discomfort, even if it's just mild awkwardness, your body goes into protection mode. You shrink. You soften. You disappear.

And here's the kicker: most of the time, the other person isn't even uncomfortable with you. They're uncomfortable with themselves. They're carrying their own stuff. But you're so tuned in to their energy that you mistake it for something you caused. Does that land?

I remember working with a woman named Sarah. She was a healer, deeply intuitive, could read a room like no one else. But she couldn't hold her own ground. Every time she shared something true, she'd immediately apologize. Every time she expressed a strong opinion, she'd soften it with "but that's just my take" or "I don't know, maybe I'm wrong." She was brilliant, but she was invisible. And she was exhausted.

When we dug into it, she realized she'd learned this pattern from her mother. Her mother was a fragile, anxious woman who couldn't handle conflict. So Sarah learned to make herself small to keep the peace. She carried that pattern into every relationship, every job, every friendship. It wasn't serving her anymore. It was suffocating her.

The Cost of Shrinking Yourself

Let me be blunt: shrinking yourself is a slow form of self-abandonment. Every time you dim your light to make someone else comfortable, you're telling yourself that their comfort matters more than your truth. And that message sinks deep into your bones. It erodes your self-trust. It hollows out your confidence. It leaves you feeling resentful, drained, and disconnected from who you actually are.

Here's what it looks like in real life:

  • You hold back your ideas in meetings because you're afraid of being judged.
  • You say yes to things you don't want to do because it's easier than dealing with someone's disappointment.
  • You edit yourself constantly, second-guessing every word before it leaves your mouth.
  • You apologize for things that aren't your fault.
  • You hide your talents, your passions, your weirdness, because you're scared of standing out.
  • You stay in relationships that don't honor you because leaving would cause ripples.

Sound familiar? Of course it does. This is the empath's dilemma. You feel everything so deeply that you've learned to prioritize other people's feelings over your own. But here's the hard truth: you can't pour from an empty cup. You can't show up for others if you've abandoned yourself. And you can't be the person you're meant to be if you're constantly shrinking.

There's a book that really helped me understand this pattern called The Gifts of Imperfection by Brené Brown. She talks about how we're wired for connection, but we often trade our authenticity for approval. It's a dangerous trade. If you haven't read it, I highly recommend it. See on Amazon (paid link)

The First Step: Stop Taking Responsibility for Other People's Feelings

This is the big one. The one that will change everything if you can really get it. You are not responsible for how other people feel. You're responsible for how you show up. You're responsible for your own integrity. But you are not responsible for managing someone else's emotions.

I know, I know. This feels wrong. It feels selfish. It feels like you're abandoning your empathic nature. But hear me out. Taking responsibility for other people's feelings is actually a form of control. It's you saying, "I can't handle your discomfort, so I'll change myself to make it go away." That's not compassion. That's fear dressed up as kindness.

True compassion is being able to hold space for someone else's discomfort without losing yourself in it. It's being able to say, "I see you're struggling, and I'm here with you, but I'm not going to abandon myself to fix it." That's a mature, grounded kind of love. And it's what the world needs more of.

So the first step is to practice this: when you feel the urge to shrink, pause. Take a breath. Ask yourself, "Is this person's discomfort mine to fix? Or is it theirs to feel?" Most of the time, it's theirs. Let them feel it. You don't have to run from it.

How to Build the Muscle of Staying Big

Stopping the shrink reflex is like building any other muscle. It takes practice. You're not going to undo years of conditioning overnight. But you can start small. Here are some practical ways to start staying big, even when it's uncomfortable.

1. Start with Your Body

Shrinking isn't just emotional. It's physical. You can feel it in your posture. Your shoulders roll forward. Your chest caves in. Your voice gets quieter. The first step to changing the pattern is to notice it happening in your body. When you feel yourself starting to shrink, consciously expand. Roll your shoulders back. Lift your sternum. Take a deep breath. Let your voice come from your belly, not your throat. This isn't about being aggressive. It's about taking up your natural space.

2. Practice Saying No Without Apologizing

This is a breakthrough. Start with small things. Someone asks you to do something you don't want to do. Instead of saying "I'm so sorry, I can't, I wish I could, but I'm just so busy," try saying "No, that doesn't work for me." That's it. No apology. No explanation. Just a clean, clear no. It will feel awkward at first. You'll want to fill the silence. Don't. Let the no stand on its own. Over time, it gets easier.

3. Speak Your Truth in Low-Stakes Situations

You don't have to start with the big stuff. Start with things that feel safe. Someone asks what movie you want to watch. Say what you actually want, not what you think they want. Someone asks for your opinion on dinner. Give it honestly. These small acts of truth-telling build the muscle. They remind you that your voice matters, even in the little things.

4. Stop Apologizing for Your Intensity

You feel things deeply. You get excited. You get passionate. You get sad. You get angry. That's not a problem. That's aliveness. The world is full of numb people who have forgotten how to feel. You're not one of them. Don't apologize for that. When you feel a big feeling coming up, let it be there. Let people see you. You don't have to be explosive or dramatic. But you don't have to hide either.

There's another book that's been incredibly helpful for this, The Highly Sensitive Person by Elaine Aron. It's the classic on this topic. She explains how sensitive people are wired differently, and why that's actually a strength, not a weakness. See on Amazon (paid link)

5. Get Comfortable with Discomfort in Others

This is the hard part. When you stop shrinking, people will react. They're used to the small version of you. They're used to you accommodating them. When you start taking up space, they might get uncomfortable. They might push back. They might say things like "What's wrong with you?" or "You've changed." Don't let that scare you back into smallness.

Their discomfort is not your problem. It's theirs. You're not being mean. You're not being selfish. You're being real. And real people sometimes make others uncomfortable because they remind them of their own un-lived lives. Let them have their discomfort. It's not yours to carry.

What About the People Who Can't Handle Your Fullness?

This is a real concern. Some relationships won't survive you taking up your space. And that's okay. Not everyone is meant to walk with you into your fullness. Some people are only comfortable with the watered-down version of you. When you stop watering yourself down, those relationships will either shift or end.

I'm not saying you should go scorched earth and cut everyone off. I'm saying you should pay attention. Notice who celebrates your growth and who tries to pull you back. Notice who feels safe and who feels draining. You don't have to make a dramatic exit. You can simply start showing up as your full self and see who sticks around. You might be surprised. Some people will rise with you. Others will fall away. Both are okay.

There's a beautiful book called Daring Greatly by Brené Brown that talks about this exact thing. She calls it "the arena." When you step into the arena of your life, you're going to get hit. But the only way to avoid getting hit is to stay out of the arena entirely. And that's no way to live. See on Amazon (paid link)

How to Stay Grounded When the Urge to Shrink Hits

The urge to shrink is going to come up. It's an old pattern, and it's wired into your nervous system. But you can learn to ride the wave instead of being swept away by it. Here's a quick practice you can use in the moment:

  • Pause. When you feel the urge to shrink, stop. Take a breath. Don't react automatically.
  • Name it. Say to yourself, "I'm feeling the urge to shrink right now." Naming it takes away some of its power.
  • Breathe into your center. Put your hand on your belly. Breathe deep into that space. Feel your feet on the ground.
  • Choose. Ask yourself, "What would it look like to stay big right now?" Then do that. Even if it's just a little bit. Even if it's just one word spoken from your truth.

This practice is simple, but it's powerful. Over time, it rewires your nervous system. You start to build a new default. Instead of automatically shrinking, you automatically pause. And in that pause, you have a choice.

The Bright Wound

I call this work "The Bright Wound" because that's what it is. Your sensitivity, your intuition, your depth of feeling - it's your brightness. But it was also the thing that got you hurt. You were too bright for a world that didn't know what to do with you. So you learned to dim yourself. But that dimming is the wound. And it's the very thing that's been holding you back.

The healing isn't about becoming less sensitive. It's about learning how to hold your sensitivity with strength. It's about learning how to be both tender and fierce. It's about learning how to feel everything without losing yourself in it. That's the work. And it's the most important work you'll ever do.

You don't have to do it alone. And you don't have to do it perfectly. You just have to start. One moment at a time. One breath at a time. One choice at a time.

You were born to take up space. You were born to be seen. You were born to be felt. Not in a way that overwhelms others, but in a way that honors yourself. That's the balance. That's the path.

And it starts right here, right now, with this choice: Are you going to shrink? Or are you going to stay?

I'm betting on you staying.
